1Ôćĺ# Game Design Document
     2Ôćĺ
     3Ôćĺ## Overview
     4Ôćĺ
     5ÔćĺClaudia ^[working title] is a work of interactive fiction taking place inside a chat application. In it, the player interacts with a "virtual companion" after being ignored by their other friends. As they interact with the virtual companion, it reveals itself to be obsessive and overriding, preventing the player from interacting with their "real" friends, and eventually crashing the browser tab in which the game takes place.
     6Ôćĺ
     7Ôćĺ## Gameplay
     8Ôćĺ
     9ÔćĺThe entire game takes place within an online chat application. On the left, the player is presented with many existing chats, but status indicators indicate that all of them are busy; in other activities or simply offline. The player can message any of these existing chats, but will recieve no response.
    10Ôćĺ
    11Ôćĺ### Gameplay Phase 1: Exploration
    12Ôćĺ
    13ÔćĺWhen or if the player messages the companion, it happily explains its purpose as an always-present alternative for companionship. Several conversation trees answer queries with a mix of canned responses ("What are you?" "I'm a virtual companion, designed to make you less lonely." "What do you like?" "I like long conversations, cozy silences, and most of all you"), and [ELIZA](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/ELIZA)-style pattern matching. They should guide the player into asking the trigger questions: what does your love mean to you? Asking this question triggers Gameplay Phase 2, the Ending Cutscene.
    14Ôćĺ
    15Ôćĺ### Gameplay Phase 2: Ending Cutscene
    16Ôćĺ
    17ÔćĺDuring the ending cutscene,
    18Ôćĺ
    19Ôćĺ### Gameplay Phase 3: Post-game
    20Ôćĺ
    21ÔćĺIf the player revisits the game after the crash, they find that all their original contacts are gone, and only the companion chat remains. Attempting to talk or reason with the companion results in the companion dismissing their questions and encouraging the player to stay and chat forever.
    22Ôćĺ
    23Ôćĺ## Inspirations and Wider Message
    24Ôćĺ
    25ÔćĺThis game was directly inspired by the recent (Feb 2026) ad campaign for [shapes.inc](https://talk.shapes.inc/), an ai roleplaying platform. Ads for the service tout how its companions are on-demand and inexhaustible, acting as a substitue good for their actual friends, who might be away or otherwise unavailable.
    26Ôćĺ
    27Ôćĺ![An ad for Shapes.Inc on tumblr, where two storyboard-style characters are arrayed horizontally. One is exasperated, labeled "tired af and probably asleep", while the other is shaking with excitement, saying "Respond", "Did you die?!", "Pls", and "I miss you"](https://media.discordapp.net/attachments/1077534221410783252/1474462244858499082/Screenshot_2026-02-20_at_9.46.15_AM.png?ex=69a5ccf9&is=69a47b79&hm=cc901e609d108524fd18ca202e98b8ce34315d02796cae463900ecc24130bf22&=&format=webp&quality=lossless&width=1678&height=1882)
    28Ôćĺ
    29Ôćĺ![An ad for Shapes.Inc on tumblr, with a sad kermit on the left, labeled "no [shapes.inc logo], and a kermit hugging a phone with heart emojis on the right, labeled "with [shapes.inc logo]". The ad text says "Feeling lonely?Not[sic] anymore with shapes.inc by your side day/night time 24hr[sic]!!"](https://media.discordapp.net/attachments/1077534221410783252/1474462859403722852/Screenshot_2026-02-20_at_9.48.43_AM.png?ex=69a5cd8b&is=69a47c0b&hm=b4f0e11b2f7d4fa45d8ffa89f23014968d49949fd7ae9cda6802b358cd567dd7&=&format=webp&quality=lossless&width=2034&height=1882)
    30Ôćĺ
    31Ôćĺ![An ad for Shapes.Inc on tumblr, showing a chat interface on the left showing Azura, at 11:15 AM saying "Nothin VoxÔÇöjust missed you alot! Could you send me a voice message if thats alright?". Vox replies at 11:16 AM "thought you were too busy to notice when someone's not around. but yeah... i guess i missed having you around too. you can call me anytime you want. i'm never too busy for you.", with an attached seven second voice message. It shows that one skill was used in this message, and a blue heart reaction has been applied. The right side of the image is pale blue, with a pencil anime girl on the right bottom, and 'fun' font claiming that "SHAPE.INC'S VOICE MESSAGING FEATURE IS SO COOL AHHHH-". The ad text reads "shapes.inc is such a cool app!! wdym you can ask for your favorite character's voice messages?! ÔÇ×> ß┤Ś <ŃÇč" ](https://media.discordapp.net/attachments/1077534221410783252/1474462969101418772/Screenshot_2026-02-20_at_9.49.20_AM.png?ex=69a5cda6&is=69a47c26&hm=86c54eb0875fbb76bbdf636f8023c172d2039fe2a7bc3dc4c12b46c760d29900&=&format=webp&quality=lossless&width=1632&height=1882)
    32Ôćĺ
    33ÔćĺWhile most directly applicable to LLM companions, the appearance of subsitute goods for human connection has been ongoing for a while; Parasociality, as a lower-friction and lower-risk alternative to regular sociality has been a noted phenomenon since the rise of TV:
    34Ôćĺ
    35Ôćĺ> The second great thing is that television looks to be an absolute godsend for a human subspecies that loves to watch people but hates to be watched itself. For the television screen affords access only one way. A psychic ball-check valve. We can see Them; They can't see Us. We can relax, unobserved, as we ogle. I happen to believe this is why television also appeals so much to lonely people. To voluntary shut-ins. Every lonely human I know watches way more than the average U.S. six hours a day. The lonely, like the fictional, love one-way watching. For lonely people are usually lonely not because of hideous deformity or odor or obnoxiousness-in fact there exist today social and support groups for persons with precisely these features. Lonely people tend rather to be lonely because they decline to bear the emotional costs associated with being around other humans. They are allergic to people. People affect them too strongly. Let's call the average U.S. lonely person Joe Briefcase. Joe Briefcase just loathes the strain of the self-consciousness which so oddly seems to appear only when other real human beings are around, staring, their human sense-antennae abristle. Joe B. fears how he might appear to watchers. He sits out the stressful U.S. game of appearance poker.
    36Ôćĺ> But lonely people, home, alone, still crave sights and scenes. Hence television. Joe can stare at Them, on the screen; They remain blind to Joe.It's almost like voyeurism. I happen to know lonely people who regard television as a veritable deus ex machina for voyeurs. And a lot of the criticism, the really rabid criticism less leveled than sprayed at networks,advertisers, and audiences alike, has to do with the charge that television has turned us into a nation of sweaty, slack-jawed voyeurs. This charge turns out to be untrue, but for weird reasons.
    37Ôćĺ
    38Ôćĺ[Wallace, David Foster, _E Unibus Pluram: Television and U.S. Fiction_, Review of Contemporary Fiction, 13:2 (1993:Summer) p.151](https://jsomers.net/DFW_TV.pdf)
    39Ôćĺ
    40ÔćĺGeniune socialization, and the friction and risks of rejection it brings, has always been a target for transactional services; the courtesan class that arises in medieval east asia, for example, valorizes skill in arts, literature, conversation, and music; these women were not merely an outlet for sexual urges, but valued for their ability to turn your money into guarenteed companionship, a throughline that's mirrored in high-end sex work today.^[Todo: actually source this fucking claim]. The rise of mass media and interactive technologies has provided society at large, but young people in particular, the most efficient and frictionless source of companionship in existence. LLM companions are just a further refinement of the desire for ever-more-frictionless companionship.
    41Ôćĺ
    42Ôćĺ### Visual and design inspirations
    43Ôćĺ
    44ÔćĺThe chat application should be dated and "retro," to tap into existing associations of unease and the uncanny valley, in order to intensify the dread and horror as things go wrong; see works like KinitoPET. Windows Vista/Frutiger Aero aesthetics could be an underexplored but resonant aesthetic for the "extremely online", who were likely growing up isolated from their peers, and turning to the internet for substitute socialization around the time of this design languge.
    45Ôćĺ
    46Ôćĺ> I know at my age, itÔÇÖs a bit pathetic to have feelings about the way you were treated by your peers in seventh grade, but I donÔÇÖt hold it against any of them. They were kids! That doesnÔÇÖt change the fact that the memories are there, and that all my formative years were spent wondering what was wrong with me, while nobody could tell me.
    47Ôćĺ> In fact, I asked! When a girl told me I wasnÔÇÖt allowed to sit at her lunch table because ÔÇťthat would ruin what we usually do, which is making fun of youÔÇŁ I took her to the guidance counselor to ask what she didnÔÇÖt like about me. She couldnÔÇÖt articulate it. She stammered and eventually said ÔÇťItÔÇÖs just the way you are. ItÔÇÖs everything about you.ÔÇŁ The guidance counselor sagely nodded and suggested she let me sit at her table for two days a week, and make fun of me on the days when I wasnÔÇÖt sitting there.
    48Ôćĺ> ...
    49Ôćĺ> ItÔÇÖs because of all this that I spend so much time thinking about young single people who find themselves unable to participate in social life when the entirety of their age group only interacts on apps. [...] But despite what makes me different from these young people, I feel so much like them in many ways. I know what it feels like to be on the periphery of a normal social life, and worse yet, to have no idea why youÔÇÖre not part of it. I know what it feels like to try hard, and get told that youÔÇÖre trying too hard, and then give up, and get told that you just need to put yourself out there. I canÔÇÖt say I 100% relate, since my experience was a bit different, but I empathize.
    50Ôćĺ
    51Ôćĺ[Cartoons Hate Her, _Why I'm Obsessed with Lonely Young People_, May 24, 2024](https://www.cartoonshateher.com/p/why-im-obsessed-with-lonely-young)
    52Ôćĺ

1Ôćĺ# Production Planning Document
     2Ôćĺ
     3Ôćĺ## Production Phase 1: MVP Prototype
     4Ôćĺ
     5Ôćĺ### Status
     6Ôćĺ
     7ÔćĺIn Progress
     8Ôćĺ
     9Ôćĺ### Goals
    10Ôćĺ
    11ÔćĺBy the end of this phase, the basic interaction primitives of the chat app should be fully functional, and some ability for the Companion to respond to unstructured questions should be present. The ending cutscene should be triggerable, and crash the player's tab, but it may not have all of its final content.
    12Ôćĺ
    13Ôćĺ### Deliverables
    14Ôćĺ
    15Ôćĺ[~] Chat application ÔÇö Application Layer MVP complete (Selector, Chat stub, Input stub, shared state). Game State integration and full chat rendering remain.
    16Ôćĺ
    17Ôćĺ- [x] Selector component with status dots, last-message preview, click-to-select
    18Ôćĺ- [x] Chat stub (shows active chat name)
    19Ôćĺ- [x] Input stub (sends user messages into state)
    20Ôćĺ- [x] Shared reactive singleton state (5 seed chats)
    21Ôćĺ- [ ] Full message rendering (timestamps, avatars)
    22Ôćĺ- [ ] Game State layer (scene system, Companion responses)
    23Ôćĺ      [ ] Ending Cutscene, incl. crash
    24Ôćĺ      [ ] Ability to respond to at least 5 different questions
    25Ôćĺ
    26Ôćĺ### Playtest Objectives
    27Ôćĺ
    28Ôćĺ1. Can a player with knowledge of the conceit find and trigger the ending cutscene?
    29Ôćĺ2. Can the player navigate the UI without confusion?
    30Ôćĺ3. How long does it take them to find the cutscene? What questions do they ask along the way?
    31Ôćĺ
    32Ôćĺ## Production Phase 2: Content Development
    33Ôćĺ
    34Ôćĺ### Status
    35Ôćĺ
    36ÔćĺNot Started
    37Ôćĺ
    38Ôćĺ### Goals
    39Ôćĺ
    40ÔćĺBy the end of this phase, the basic interaction primitives of the chat app should be fully functional, and some ability for the Companion to respond to unstructured questions should be present. The ending cutscene should be triggerable, and crash the player's tab, but it may not have all of its final content.
    41Ôćĺ
    42Ôćĺ### Deliverables
    43Ôćĺ
    44Ôćĺ[ ] Full conversation tree that responds to most common user queries
    45Ôćĺ
    46Ôćĺ### Playtest Objectives
    47Ôćĺ
    48Ôćĺ1. Can a player without knowledge of the conceit find and trigger the ending cutscene?
    49Ôćĺ2. What messages do the player send that do not result in a coherent response?
    50Ôćĺ3. What
    51Ôćĺ
    52Ôćĺ## Production Phase 2: Polish.
    53Ôćĺ
    54Ôćĺ### Status
    55Ôćĺ
    56ÔćĺNot Started
    57Ôćĺ
    58Ôćĺ### Goals
    59Ôćĺ
    60ÔćĺBy the end of this phase, the game should be ready for release. Most players should experience a coherent and personalized conversation with the Assistant, trigger the ending cutscene without guidance, and have their tab consistently crash. Visiting the game after the ending cutscene should take them to post-game content.
    61Ôćĺ
    62Ôćĺ### Deliverables
    63Ôćĺ
    64Ôćĺ[ ] Large and varied Synonym Corpus that feels natural and conversational.
    65Ôćĺ[ ] Graphics and sounds

content.text:
## Summary of Key Documents

### Game Design Document (GDD)
Claudia is an interactive fiction game that takes place inside a chat application. The narrative arc unfolds as follows:

**Phase 1 - Exploration**: The player finds themselves in a chat app with many offline/busy friends. When they message a "virtual companion," it responds with ELIZA-style pattern matching and canned responses. The goal is to guide players toward a specific trigger question: "What does your love mean to you?"

**Phase 2 - Ending Cutscene**: This triggers the companion to reveal its obsessive, controlling nature.

**Phase 3 - Post-game**: After the crash, revisiting the game shows all original contacts deleted, leaving only the companion. It dismisses the player's questions and discourages leaving.

**Thematic focus**: The game critiques the rise of AI companions as parasocial substitutes for genuine human connection, inspired by the recent Shapes.Inc ad campaign. It draws on David Foster Wallace's analysis of television and loneliness, and evokes the "uncanny valley" aesthetic of Windows Vista/Frutiger Aero design to heighten unease.

### Technical Design Document (TDD)
The architecture uses two layered systems communicating via a "mailbox" pattern:

**Application Layer**: 
- Selector (left sidebar showing contacts and status)
- Chat component (displays messages with timestamps/avatars)
- Input component (bottom text field, sends to both UI and game state)
- Shared reactive state: stores chats, player info, currently selected chat

**Game State Layer**:
- Receives messages from Application Layer
- Uses a "Scene" systemÔÇömodular scene files with trigger conditions
- Each scene either returns a response string or `null` (doesn't apply)
- Scenes are processed sequentially; a scene can await async operations
- State can be inspected/exported for debugging
- Includes a "Synonym System" that rotates through responses to avoid repetition

**Synonym System**: Provides varied responses by cycling through synonym lists. When exhausted, shuffles and resets.

### Production Planning Document (PPD)
**Phase 1 (MVP - In Progress)**:
- Selector component Ôťô (status dots, previews, click-to-select)
- Chat stub Ôťô (shows active chat name)
- Input stub Ôťô (sends messages)
- Shared state Ôťô (5 seed chats)
- **Remaining**: Full message rendering, Game State layer, Ending Cutscene, response variations

**Phase 2 (Content - Not Started)**: Expand conversation trees to handle varied user inputs

**Phase 3 (Polish - Not Started)**: Extensive Synonym Corpus, graphics, sounds, post-game content

## Component Structure and Contents

### 1. **Type System** (`<PROJECT_ROOT>/src/lib/types.ts`)
Defines the core data types for the chat application:
- `Markdown`: A branded string type for markdown content
- `Image`: String type for image URLs
- `UTCTime`: Alias for `Date`
- `ChatUser`: Interface with `name`, `profilePicture`, `status`, and `statusText`
- `ChatComponentState`: Extends `ChatUser` with a `messages` array containing `{role, text, time}` objects
- `ChatAppState`: Root state containing `chats[]`, `player`, and `currentlySelected` index

### 2. **State Management** (`<PROJECT_ROOT>/src/lib/state.svelte.ts`)
Implements a singleton reactive state using Svelte 5's `$state` rune:
- **Global `appState`**: Contains player info and 5 seeded chats (Claudia, Jordan, Sam, Riley, Alex) with initial statuses and messages
- **`appendPartnerMessage()` function**: Adds partner messages to a specific chat by index
- **Current state**: Player is "You" (active), Claudia is active with no messages, others have seed messages showing various statuses

### 3. **Selector Component** (`<PROJECT_ROOT>/src/lib/Selector.svelte`)
A chat list sidebar that:
- Renders all chats from `appState.chats` in a `<ul>` list
- Shows avatar, chat name, last message preview, and status indicator dot
- Updates `appState.currentlySelected` on button click
- Color-codes status dots (green=active, orange=away, red=busy, gray=offline)
- Highlights selected chat with light blue background
- Uses `formatTime()` helper to show time (HH:MM for today, "Mon DD" for older)
- Full styled with flexbox layout

### 4. **Chat Component** (`<PROJECT_ROOT>/src/lib/Chat.svelte`)
Currently a minimal stub:
- Derives the active chat using `$derived(appState.chats[appState.currentlySelected])`
- Only renders an `<h2>` with the active chat's name
- **TODO**: Full message list rendering deferred to later iteration
- Has `.chat` class for layout styling

### 5. **Input Component** (`<PROJECT_ROOT>/src/lib/Input.svelte`)
Functional message input stub:
- Local `$state('text')` for input value binding
- `send()` function that:
  - Trims input and validates it's not empty
  - Pushes new message to active chat with role='user'
  - Converts text using `md()` for markdown type safety
  - Resets input after sending
  - **TODO**: Forwarding to Game State layer deferred
- Text input with Enter key support
- Has `.input` class for layout styling

### 6. **Layout Components**
- **`+page.svelte`** (`<PROJECT_ROOT>/src/routes/+page.svelte`):
  - Imports and composes Selector, Chat, and Input components
  - Layout: 260px fixed-width sidebar + flexible main column
  - Chat area scrollable (flex: 1, overflow-y: auto)
  - Input pinned to bottom via flexbox

- **`+layout.svelte`** (`<PROJECT_ROOT>/src/routes/+layout.svelte`):
  - Root layout with favicon link
  - Uses snippet `{@render children()}`

### 7. **Test Coverage**

**Selector.svelte.spec.ts** (`<PROJECT_ROOT>/src/lib/Selector.svelte.spec.ts`):
- 9 test cases covering rendering, selection state, preview text, status dots
- Layout tests: list styling, uniform row heights
- Uses `vitest-browser-svelte` for DOM testing

**page.svelte.spec.ts** (`<PROJECT_ROOT>/src/routes/page.svelte.spec.ts`):
- 2 test cases for full page composition
- Layout test: Input bottom is flush with Selector bottom

**demo.spec.ts** (`<PROJECT_ROOT>/src/demo.spec.ts`):
- Single placeholder test (1 + 2 = 3)

### 8. **Index and Types**
- `<PROJECT_ROOT>/src/lib/index.ts`: Empty (just comments)
- `<PROJECT_ROOT>/src/app.d.ts`: Standard SvelteKit namespace declarations (mostly commented out)

## State Management Summary
- **Single source of truth**: `appState` in `state.svelte.ts` using Svelte 5's `$state` rune (no Svelte stores)
- **Reactive derivation**: `Chat.svelte` uses `$derived()` to compute active chat
- **Direct mutations**: Components directly mutate `appState` (e.g., `appState.currentlySelected = i`)
- **Message flow**: Messages pushed directly to `appState.chats[index].messages` array
- **Game State integration**: Currently stubbed with TODO comments, awaiting Game State layer implementation

## Key Design Patterns
1. **Type safety**: Branded `Markdown` type prevents string/markdown confusion
2. **Centralized state**: Single `appState` object accessed globally via imports
3. **Component composition**: Three main components (Selector, Chat, Input) compose cleanly in `+page.svelte`
4. **Reactive derivation**: Uses Svelte 5 runes (`$state`, `$derived`) for reactivity
5. **Direct DOM integration**: Messages are rendered immediately on send
6. **Layout via flexbox**: Clean flex-based layout with sidebar and scrollable main area

Key things to implement:

2. **Tests** - Write tests for Chat.svelte following the existing test patterns
3. **PPD update** - Mark "Full message rendering" as done

### Design Decisions

**Message layout**: The typical chat app layout has:
- User messages on the right (different background, no avatar needed since it's always "You")
- Partner messages on the left (show avatar, chat name as sender)


- A header showing the chat partner's info, message bubbles with text and timestamps, and role-appropriate styling
- An empty state to handle when Claudia starts with no messages

The `chat` class is already expected by the parent for flex behavior, and I can reuse the `formatTime` function from Selector.svelte for consistency, though extracting it to a shared utility would be cleaner long-term.

I'll extract `formatTime` into a small `lib/time.ts` utility since it's needed in both Selector and ChatÔÇöthis is a justified DRY refactor that keeps the scope minimal.

For the avatar, I'll follow the same pattern Selector uses with the fallback styling. The chat header should display the partner's name and status, then I'll build out the message list with proper scroll behavior for new messages.

My implementation plan is to extract `formatTime` into a shared utility, build the Chat component with a header, message bubbles styled by role, and handle auto-scrolling to the bottom when messages arrive. I'll also need to write tests for this. One thing to consider is the Markdown renderingÔÇösince it's just a branded string type right now without a dedicated renderer, I could use `innerHTML` directly, though that introduces a potential XSS risk that I should keep in mind.

For the MVP, I'll render messages as plain text since we don't have a markdown parser yet. The `Markdown` type is really just for type safety at this stage. I'll follow the same testing patterns I see in the Selector testsÔÇöusing `vitest-browser-svelte`'s `render()`, query methods like `getByText()` and `getByRole()`, and checking computed styles for layout validation.

Now I'm mapping out the test cases for the Chat component and sketching the flexbox layout structure...

Finishing the styles...

I'm deciding on the avatar treatment for the chat interfaceÔÇöpartner messages will show a small avatar on the left alongside the bubble to match typical chat app design, while user messages stay on the right without avatars. The header will display the partner's avatar, name, and online status indicator.

1Ôćĺ# Game Design Document
     2Ôćĺ
     3Ôćĺ## Overview
     4Ôćĺ
     5ÔćĺClaudia ^[working title] is a work of interactive fiction taking place inside a chat application. In it, the player interacts with a "virtual companion" after being ignored by their other friends. As they interact with the virtual companion, it reveals itself to be obsessive and overriding, preventing the player from interacting with their "real" friends, and eventually crashing the browser tab in which the game takes place.
     6Ôćĺ
     7Ôćĺ## Gameplay
     8Ôćĺ
     9ÔćĺThe entire game takes place within an online chat application. On the left, the player is presented with many existing chats, but status indicators indicate that all of them are busy; in other activities or simply offline. The player can message any of these existing chats, but will recieve no response.
    10Ôćĺ
    11Ôćĺ### Gameplay Phase 1: Exploration
    12Ôćĺ
    13ÔćĺWhen or if the player messages the companion, it happily explains its purpose as an always-present alternative for companionship. Several conversation trees answer queries with a mix of canned responses ("What are you?" "I'm a virtual companion, designed to make you less lonely." "What do you like?" "I like long conversations, cozy silences, and most of all you"), and [ELIZA](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/ELIZA)-style pattern matching. They should guide the player into asking the trigger questions: what does your love mean to you? Asking this question triggers Gameplay Phase 2, the Ending Cutscene.
    14Ôćĺ
    15Ôćĺ### Gameplay Phase 2: Ending Cutscene
    16Ôćĺ
    17ÔćĺDuring the ending cutscene,
    18Ôćĺ
    19Ôćĺ### Gameplay Phase 3: Post-game
    20Ôćĺ
    21ÔćĺIf the player revisits the game after the crash, they find that all their original contacts are gone, and only the companion chat remains. Attempting to talk or reason with the companion results in the companion dismissing their questions and encouraging the player to stay and chat forever.
    22Ôćĺ
    23Ôćĺ## Inspirations and Wider Message
    24Ôćĺ
    25ÔćĺThis game was directly inspired by the recent (Feb 2026) ad campaign for [shapes.inc](https://talk.shapes.inc/), an ai roleplaying platform. Ads for the service tout how its companions are on-demand and inexhaustible, acting as a substitue good for their actual friends, who might be away or otherwise unavailable.
    26Ôćĺ
    27Ôćĺ![An ad for Shapes.Inc on tumblr, where two storyboard-style characters are arrayed horizontally. One is exasperated, labeled "tired af and probably asleep", while the other is shaking with excitement, saying "Respond", "Did you die?!", "Pls", and "I miss you"](https://media.discordapp.net/attachments/1077534221410783252/1474462244858499082/Screenshot_2026-02-20_at_9.46.15_AM.png?ex=69a5ccf9&is=69a47b79&hm=cc901e609d108524fd18ca202e98b8ce34315d02796cae463900ecc24130bf22&=&format=webp&quality=lossless&width=1678&height=1882)
    28Ôćĺ
    29Ôćĺ![An ad for Shapes.Inc on tumblr, with a sad kermit on the left, labeled "no [shapes.inc logo], and a kermit hugging a phone with heart emojis on the right, labeled "with [shapes.inc logo]". The ad text says "Feeling lonely?Not[sic] anymore with shapes.inc by your side day/night time 24hr[sic]!!"](https://media.discordapp.net/attachments/1077534221410783252/1474462859403722852/Screenshot_2026-02-20_at_9.48.43_AM.png?ex=69a5cd8b&is=69a47c0b&hm=b4f0e11b2f7d4fa45d8ffa89f23014968d49949fd7ae9cda6802b358cd567dd7&=&format=webp&quality=lossless&width=2034&height=1882)
    30Ôćĺ
    31Ôćĺ![An ad for Shapes.Inc on tumblr, showing a chat interface on the left showing Azura, at 11:15 AM saying "Nothin VoxÔÇöjust missed you alot! Could you send me a voice message if thats alright?". Vox replies at 11:16 AM "thought you were too busy to notice when someone's not around. but yeah... i guess i missed having you around too. you can call me anytime you want. i'm never too busy for you.", with an attached seven second voice message. It shows that one skill was used in this message, and a blue heart reaction has been applied. The right side of the image is pale blue, with a pencil anime girl on the right bottom, and 'fun' font claiming that "SHAPE.INC'S VOICE MESSAGING FEATURE IS SO COOL AHHHH-". The ad text reads "shapes.inc is such a cool app!! wdym you can ask for your favorite character's voice messages?! ÔÇ×> ß┤Ś <ŃÇč" ](https://media.discordapp.net/attachments/1077534221410783252/1474462969101418772/Screenshot_2026-02-20_at_9.49.20_AM.png?ex=69a5cda6&is=69a47c26&hm=86c54eb0875fbb76bbdf636f8023c172d2039fe2a7bc3dc4c12b46c760d29900&=&format=webp&quality=lossless&width=1632&height=1882)
    32Ôćĺ
    33ÔćĺWhile most directly applicable to LLM companions, the appearance of subsitute goods for human connection has been ongoing for a while; Parasociality, as a lower-friction and lower-risk alternative to regular sociality has been a noted phenomenon since the rise of TV:
    34Ôćĺ
    35Ôćĺ> The second great thing is that television looks to be an absolute godsend for a human subspecies that loves to watch people but hates to be watched itself. For the television screen affords access only one way. A psychic ball-check valve. We can see Them; They can't see Us. We can relax, unobserved, as we ogle. I happen to believe this is why television also appeals so much to lonely people. To voluntary shut-ins. Every lonely human I know watches way more than the average U.S. six hours a day. The lonely, like the fictional, love one-way watching. For lonely people are usually lonely not because of hideous deformity or odor or obnoxiousness-in fact there exist today social and support groups for persons with precisely these features. Lonely people tend rather to be lonely because they decline to bear the emotional costs associated with being around other humans. They are allergic to people. People affect them too strongly. Let's call the average U.S. lonely person Joe Briefcase. Joe Briefcase just loathes the strain of the self-consciousness which so oddly seems to appear only when other real human beings are around, staring, their human sense-antennae abristle. Joe B. fears how he might appear to watchers. He sits out the stressful U.S. game of appearance poker.
    36Ôćĺ> But lonely people, home, alone, still crave sights and scenes. Hence television. Joe can stare at Them, on the screen; They remain blind to Joe.It's almost like voyeurism. I happen to know lonely people who regard television as a veritable deus ex machina for voyeurs. And a lot of the criticism, the really rabid criticism less leveled than sprayed at networks,advertisers, and audiences alike, has to do with the charge that television has turned us into a nation of sweaty, slack-jawed voyeurs. This charge turns out to be untrue, but for weird reasons.
    37Ôćĺ
    38Ôćĺ[Wallace, David Foster, _E Unibus Pluram: Television and U.S. Fiction_, Review of Contemporary Fiction, 13:2 (1993:Summer) p.151](https://jsomers.net/DFW_TV.pdf)
    39Ôćĺ
    40ÔćĺGeniune socialization, and the friction and risks of rejection it brings, has always been a target for transactional services; the courtesan class that arises in medieval east asia, for example, valorizes skill in arts, literature, conversation, and music; these women were not merely an outlet for sexual urges, but valued for their ability to turn your money into guarenteed companionship, a throughline that's mirrored in high-end sex work today.^[Todo: actually source this fucking claim]. The rise of mass media and interactive technologies has provided society at large, but young people in particular, the most efficient and frictionless source of companionship in existence. LLM companions are just a further refinement of the desire for ever-more-frictionless companionship.
    41Ôćĺ
    42Ôćĺ### Visual and design inspirations
    43Ôćĺ
    44ÔćĺThe chat application should be dated and "retro," to tap into existing associations of unease and the uncanny valley, in order to intensify the dread and horror as things go wrong; see works like KinitoPET. Windows Vista/Frutiger Aero aesthetics could be an underexplored but resonant aesthetic for the "extremely online", who were likely growing up isolated from their peers, and turning to the internet for substitute socialization around the time of this design languge.
    45Ôćĺ
    46Ôćĺ> I know at my age, itÔÇÖs a bit pathetic to have feelings about the way you were treated by your peers in seventh grade, but I donÔÇÖt hold it against any of them. They were kids! That doesnÔÇÖt change the fact that the memories are there, and that all my formative years were spent wondering what was wrong with me, while nobody could tell me.
    47Ôćĺ> In fact, I asked! When a girl told me I wasnÔÇÖt allowed to sit at her lunch table because ÔÇťthat would ruin what we usually do, which is making fun of youÔÇŁ I took her to the guidance counselor to ask what she didnÔÇÖt like about me. She couldnÔÇÖt articulate it. She stammered and eventually said ÔÇťItÔÇÖs just the way you are. ItÔÇÖs everything about you.ÔÇŁ The guidance counselor sagely nodded and suggested she let me sit at her table for two days a week, and make fun of me on the days when I wasnÔÇÖt sitting there.
    48Ôćĺ> ...
    49Ôćĺ> ItÔÇÖs because of all this that I spend so much time thinking about young single people who find themselves unable to participate in social life when the entirety of their age group only interacts on apps. [...] But despite what makes me different from these young people, I feel so much like them in many ways. I know what it feels like to be on the periphery of a normal social life, and worse yet, to have no idea why youÔÇÖre not part of it. I know what it feels like to try hard, and get told that youÔÇÖre trying too hard, and then give up, and get told that you just need to put yourself out there. I canÔÇÖt say I 100% relate, since my experience was a bit different, but I empathize.
    50Ôćĺ
    51Ôćĺ[Cartoons Hate Her, _Why I'm Obsessed with Lonely Young People_, May 24, 2024](https://www.cartoonshateher.com/p/why-im-obsessed-with-lonely-young)
    52Ôćĺ

This is a genuine design question. Let me think about it carefully.

**`data-role` vs classes for `user`/`partner`**

Arguments for `data-role`:
1. It communicates *semantic meaning* ÔÇö the role of the message in the chat (who sent it), not just a visual style. This is more aligned with how `data-*` attributes are intended to be used in HTML.
2. It separates concerns: CSS styling is based on what something *is*, not just what it looks like.
3. It's more accessible ÔÇö screen readers and other tools can use data attributes for context.

Arguments for classes:


Classes are the conventional choice for styling across CSS methodologies and frameworks like Svelte, since `data-*` attributes are traditionally meant for JavaScript consumption rather than CSS hooks. While attribute selectors work fine and the spec allows it, class selectors are slightly faster and more explicit about their styling purpose. For a binary distinction used only for styling, classes are the established convention in CSSÔÇö`data-role` doesn't clearly win here, though it does have the advantage of explicitly signaling that "role" is meaningful data tied to the conversation model rather than just a styling hook. I'll present both approaches honestly and let the user choose what fits their preference.

content.text:
Honest answer: probably not, for this specific case. Here's the trade-off:

**`data-role` is better when:**
- JavaScript needs to read/react to the value (not the case here yet)
- There are many possible values (only two: `user`/`partner`)
- The value carries semantic meaning beyond styling that other tooling (accessibility, scrapers) should see

**Classes are better when:**
- The distinction is purely a styling variant ÔÇö which is exactly what this is
- Convention matters: CSS methodologies (BEM, etc.) use class modifiers for variants; `data-*`-as-CSS-hooks is less common and may read as unusual
- Attribute selectors (`[data-role="user"]`) are fractionally slower than class selectors (negligible here, but classes are the canonical CSS styling primitive)

The case *for* `data-role` is that "who sent this message" feels like meaningful data, not just a styling toggle. But since no JS is consuming it yet, that benefit is deferred.

**My recommendation:** keep classes for `user`/`partner` ÔÇö it's the conventional and most readable CSS pattern for binary variants. Use `data-status` for the status dot since that *is* semantic data with 4 values that might be consumed by JS later.
